8E4372EB0DB8|The transport hummed to a stop, and Marnette's hand went to the door handle before the announcement finished. Her boots left red dust on the gray floor mat—fine, persistent powder that found its way into every crevice of the colony, no matter how sophisticated the air filtration. She brushed at the residue automatically, though she knew it was futile. Mars dust was eternal. It coated everything eventually, a constant reminder that even inside their protected habitats, they were still on Mars. Still living on borrowed time in a place that had never been meant for human life.

A0AF4E851C65|"Residence 7B, Master Frost's quarters," the transport's voice said, calm and genderless. "Caregiver Marnette Croft, please proceed to the main entrance."

C19C06B51E40|Marnette straightened her gray tunic, checking that her prayer beads were properly tucked beneath the collar. The beads felt smooth against her skin—familiar, grounding. She had worn them since her Coming of Age ceremony, sixteen years ago now. Elom guide my steps, she thought, the prayer automatic as breathing. Elom guide my hands to serve.

FB9C6F812540|The journey from the salve quarters to Residence 7B had taken forty-seven minutes through the colony's transit tubes. Marnette had never been assigned to the Master sector before. Her previous assignments had all been in the salve districts or neutral zones—the medical centers, elderly care facilities, hospice wings where salves went to die. But a Master dying... that was rare enough to be talked about for years. Masters lived eight centuries, sometimes longer. Their deaths were events that reshaped the colony's power structure, required year-long mourning periods, were recorded in histories studied for generations.

CD240D6C0AA7|And she had been chosen as Timon Frost's caregiver. Specifically requested. The assignment notice had arrived three days ago, delivered by a messenger in the Master's household staff uniform. Marnette had read it twice, certain there was some mistake. She was skilled—eight years of service had taught her everything about comfort care, pain management, helping people face the end with dignity. But she was still a salve. Still twenty-six, with at most thirty-four years left to serve. Masters usually chose older caregivers for final assignments—people who had seen decades of service, who understood history's weight, who wouldn't be intimidated by proximity to power.

DD6DAC913B8C|But the notice was real. And now here she was, her heart beating too fast, hands clammy despite the transport's climate control. She took a deep breath, centering herself. This was her duty. Her calling. Elom had given her this opportunity, and she would not fail.

1A74014BAF91|The door slid open before she reached it. Not the transport door—her door. Beyond it stretched a corridor that made her catch her breath. Not because it was grand—Masters' corridors were always grand—but because of the light. Real sunlight, filtered through the dome's polarized glass, cast long shadows across polished marble floors. Her own corridor in the salve quarters had the same artificial lighting as every other public space—steady, reliable, but flat. This light had depth. C6F206865E67|The corridor was a work of art. Marble patterned with gold and copper veins, walls covered in tapestries depicting secular scenes from the colony's founding—Musk's arrival, first habitat construction, agricultural domes. History, but not the sacred history she had grown up with.

650A764793D7|A woman stood waiting beside an ornate door. Not a Master—her clothing too simple, posture too deferential. D8E8FE2D6BB4|"You must be Marnette," the woman said. Her voice was soft, pitched not to carry. "I'm Elara. Master Frost's household manager."

5B378983EF6E|"Elara," Marnette said, inclining her head. "It's an honor to be chosen for this assignment."

77E5A3AB74AA|Elara's expression didn't change. "An honor, yes. And a challenge." She gestured toward the door. "Master Frost is awake. He's been expecting you."

47B1C284F336|Marnette followed, steps silent on marble. The contrast between her worn boots and pristine floor made her self-conscious, but Elara didn't seem to notice. 9DCCAD3EE04F|"Has he been difficult?" Marnette asked. "The briefing mentioned his condition is deteriorating."

644D181EAB8B|"Difficult isn't the word I'd use." Elara paused at the door, her hand hovering over the panel. "Master Frost is... thoughtful. Even now. Especially now, perhaps." She lowered her voice. FCAA165E5408|Marnette felt warmth spread through her chest. Elder Maris had been difficult—painful, demanding, but ultimately rewarding. E6A8DA3B50C1|"I heard about Elder Maris's passing," Elara said, voice softening. "She was well-respected. Many attended her service."

2DD2860A5906|"She was at peace," Marnette said. "In the end, she was ready. She had lived a full life."

7047B04185B0|"Fifty-eight years," Elara nodded. "A good long life. For a salve."

7990788729E9|The phrase hung in air between them, unspoken but understood. A good long life. For a salve. F7D22A27DF75|"Master Frost has lived seven hundred ninety-three years," Elara said, voice returning to professional neutrality. "Seven hundred ninety-three years. Can you imagine that? He remembers the founding. He knew people who knew Elon Musk himself."

C23517339D8D|Marnette couldn't imagine it. The span of time was beyond comprehension. FF26E13593BA|"Your duty, and more." Elara's fingers danced across the panel. The door slid open silently. "He doesn't have much time left. The physicians say weeks, perhaps a month if he's fortunate."

CDD5D123D99B|The door opened into a space that took Marnette's breath away. This wasn't just a room; it was a residence that occupied an entire level of the habitat. The room was furnished with pieces that looked like they had been brought from Earth—dark wood furniture that couldn't possibly be manufactured on Mars, fabrics in colors that didn't appear in any salve clothing, art on the walls that was abstract and personal rather than institutional.

CF839D8404E7|But what struck her most was the view. 29BC3834648E|And in the center of all this luxury, all this space, all this light, was a bed. And in the bed was a man who looked like he might be made of the same red dust outside, fragile and ancient and somehow eternal.

429477FF23F8|Timon Frost.

37330FC211DE|He didn't look like a dying man. Not at first. He looked ancient, certainly—his face was a roadmap of wrinkles, his skin thin as parchment, his hair white as fresh snow. He was propped up against pillows that looked like they were filled with something softer than the standard foam, covered in fabric that shimmered slightly in the sunlight. 4A80D6E57A92|The room itself was divided into functional areas. 05B8D8E3237B|But it was the man in the bed who commanded her attention. Timon Frost. She had heard his name, of course—everyone had. 82C46C1740C6|Yet his eyes... his eyes were something else entirely. 0DF3981C0F09|"Master Frost," Marnette said, bowing deeply. "I am Marnette Croft. Elara said you were expecting me."

70BDE2F7E3CA|"Marnette Croft," he repeated. His voice was dry, rustling like dead leaves, but each word was precise. "Come closer. Let me see you."

09DB217718B7|She approached the bed, her professional calm settling over her like a familiar cloak. AFD40C41FB85|As she drew closer, she could see more details. The skin on his face was so thin that she could see the skull beneath it, the bones sharp and defined. 6095B06DF6C0|"You're younger than I expected," Frost said. "They told me you were skilled, but I pictured someone older."

0BE49708029A|"I'm twenty-six, Master Frost. I've been a caregiver for eight years."

9FBDFB522154|"Eight years." He studied her face, and Marnette had the strange feeling that he was looking past her skin, past her bones, into the very essence of her being. "That's a long time for someone so young. Do you enjoy the work?"

CCEA0DAB147A|"It's my calling," she said, and it was true. "Elom gives each of us purpose. Mine is to comfort those who need comfort."

0F3DA0D98109|"Elom." Frost's lips curved into something that might have been a smile, if his face had remembered how. "You're faithful, then. Truly faithful, not just performing the rituals."

52D9585757C4|"Elom designed us for our roles, Master Frost. To question that would be to question Elom's wisdom."

8F9D1387EF91|"Would it?" He shifted slightly, and Marnette moved to help him, but he held up a hand. "No. Don't. I can still move, even if it costs me." He took a breath, and she heard the rattle in his chest. "Tell me about Elder Maris. They said you were with her at the end."

AAF5CE516577|"We're all born for our work, Master Frost. That's Elom's design."

951B6FB437C1|"Is it?" He closed his eyes for a moment, and when he opened them again, some of the intensity had faded, replaced by the weariness she expected from a dying man. "I'm tired now. Elara will show you to your room. We'll talk more tomorrow."

6D1745D3E470|"Do you ever dream of Earth?"

9FD0975DCE39|"I dream of serving Elom, Master Frost," she said, which was true. "Earth is... history."

99A39BF38B58|"Is it?" His eyes were heavy now, drifting toward sleep. "History has a way of becoming present. Remember that."

824B69E20CB1|"He likes you," Elara said, leading Marnette toward another door. "That's rare. Master Frost doesn't like many people."

2C2C58EBB310|Elara stopped walking. "Did he?"

05CAF1C31068|"Is that... unusual?"

2CB71A8A94D9|"Your belongings were brought from your quarters," Elara said, gesturing to a small bag on the bed. "Dinner is at seventh hour. Master Frost usually takes his meal in his room, but you'll eat in the small kitchen unless he summons you."

34F81694D3AA|"Thank you, Elara. This is more than I expected."

352A9CD837E0|"Master Frost provides for those who serve him well." Elara paused at the door, her hand on the frame. "One more thing, Marnette. This assignment... it's not like the others. FC139D9DB5C6|"I need to serve, Master Frost. That's different."

F54F20AD9D82|"Is it?" He struggled to sit up, and Marnette moved to help him, plumping the pillows behind his back. "Thank you. You have gentle hands. Most people are rough with me now. They think I can't feel it."

AE041D26177D|"You can feel everything," Marnette said. "That's what makes dying hard."

54B2DCF58D37|"Stories. The old stories. Before they were cleaned up. Before they were made safe." He let go of her hand and sank back against the pillows. "Now go. Eat your dinner. Sleep. Tomorrow will be a long day."

AB1BE065350B|One entry, dated just before the colony's founding, talked about "biological castes" and "lifespan limitations" as if they were engineering problems to be solved rather than divine design. BEE1ACB9D0FD|The most disturbing entries were from the early years of the colony, when the Church of Elom was being established. BADC9CA82958|"No," she said aloud, her voice sharp in the quiet room. "That's wrong. That's dangerous thinking."

F31CB3BF0F0E|She returned to the bed and lay down, pulling the blanket over her shoulders. The device was on the bedside table, its screen dark. She should turn it over to the authorities. She should report Frost's strange questions. She should—

C92A59988CD2|Still, as she finally drifted toward sleep, one thought remained clear: this assignment was not like the others. And when it was over, she would not be the same. The question was not whether she would change, but how much she would already have changed without even realizing it was happening. She, who had always prided herself on acceptance, on finding peace in her designated role, was now harboring thoughts that felt like betrayal. Thoughts that questioned the very foundation of everything she believed, everything she was, everything she had ever been taught to value. And in the quiet darkness of her room, with the red dust of Mars waiting just beyond the habitat walls, Marnette understood that some changes, once begun, could not be stopped. Some questions, once asked, could not be unasked. And some truths, once glimpsed, could never be unseen again. The weight of that knowledge settled over her like the fine red dust that covered everything in the colony—subtle at first, almost unnoticeable, but gradually accumulating until it changed the very landscape of her mind. She would wake tomorrow and go about her duties with the same professionalism, the same devotion, the same care she had always provided. But something fundamental had shifted, something deep within her that would never be quite the same. The assignment had only just begun, and already she understood that whatever happened with Master Frost, however it ended, she would not emerge unchanged. The knowledge itself was a kind of death—the death of the person she had been when she arrived that morning.
